content:
# Genesis Chapter 8 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 5r–5v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_008_page_001.jpg, GEN_008_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0009

---

**8:1** And God remembered [וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים] Noah [אֶת-נֹחַ], and all the living things [וְאֵת כָּל-הַחַיָּה] and all the beasts [וְאֶת-כָּל-הַבְּהֵמָה] that were with him in the ark [אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בַּתֵּבָה]; and God caused a wind to pass [וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ] over the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ], and the waters subsided [וַיָּשֹׁכּוּ הַמָּיִם].

**8:2** And the fountains of the deep were closed [וַיִּסָּכְרוּ מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם] and the windows of the heavens [וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמָיִם], and the rain was restrained [וַיִּכָּלֵא הַגֶּשֶׁם] from the heavens [מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם].

**8:3** And the waters returned [וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם] from upon the earth [מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ], going and returning [הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב]; and the waters decreased [וַיַּחְסְרוּ הַמַּיִם] at the end of [מִקְצֵה] one hundred and fifty days [חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם].

**8:4** And the ark rested [וַתָּנַח הַתֵּבָה] in the seventh month [בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי], on the seventeenth day of the month [בְּשִׁבְעָה-עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ], upon the mountains of Ararat [עַל הָרֵי אֲרָרָט].

**8:5** And the waters were going and decreasing [וְהַמַּיִם הָיוּ הָלוֹךְ וְחָסוֹר] until the tenth month [עַד הַחֹדֶשׁ הָעֲשִׂירִי]; in the tenth month [בָּעֲשִׂירִי], on the first of the month [בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ], the tops of the mountains appeared [נִרְאוּ רָאשֵׁי הֶהָרִים].

**8:6** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] at the end of forty days [מִקֵּץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם], that Noah opened [וַיִּפְתַּח נֹחַ] the window of the ark [אֶת-חַלּוֹן הַתֵּבָה] that he had made [אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה].

**8:7** And he sent out [וַיְשַׁלַּח] the raven [אֶת-הָעֹרֵב], and it went out [וַיֵּצֵא] going and returning [יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב] until the waters dried up [עַד-יְבֹשֶׁת הַמַּיִם] from upon the earth [מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ].

**8:8** And he sent out [וַיְשַׁלַּח] the dove [אֶת-הַיּוֹנָה] from with him [מֵאִתּוֹ], to see [לִרְאוֹת] whether the waters had lessened [הֲקַלּוּ הַמַּיִם] from upon the face of the ground [מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה].

**8:9** But the dove found no resting-place [וְלֹא-מָצְאָה הַיּוֹנָה מָנוֹחַ] for the sole of her foot [לְכַף-רַגְלָהּ], and she returned to him [וַתָּשָׁב אֵלָיו] to the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה], for water was upon the face of all the earth [כִּי-מַיִם עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ]; and he stretched out his hand [וַיִּשְׁלַח יָדוֹ] and took her [וַיִּקָּחֶהָ], and he brought her in [וַיָּבֵא אֹתָהּ] to him [אֵלָיו] into the ark [אֶל-הַתֵּבָה].

**8:10** And he waited [וַיָּחֶל] again yet seven days [עוֹד שִׁבְעַת יָמִים אֲחֵרִים], and again he sent out [וַיֹּסֶף שַׁלַּח] the dove [אֶת-הַיּוֹנָה] from the ark [מִן-הַתֵּבָה].

**8:11** And the dove came [וַתָּבֹא אֵלָיו הַיּוֹנָה] to him at evening time [לְעֵת עֶרֶב], and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — a freshly plucked olive leaf [עֲלֵה-זַיִת טָרָף] in her mouth [בְּפִיהָ]; and Noah knew [וַיֵּדַע נֹחַ] that the waters had lessened [כִּי-קַלּוּ הַמַּיִם] from upon the earth [מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ].

**8:12** And he waited [וַיִּיָּחֶל] again yet seven days [עוֹד שִׁבְעַת יָמִים אֲחֵרִים], and he sent out [וַיְשַׁלַּח] the dove [אֶת-הַיּוֹנָה], and it did not return [וְלֹא-יָסְפָה שׁוּב-אֵלָיו] to him again [עוֹד].

**8:13** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] in the six hundred and first year [בְּאַחַת וְשֵׁשׁ-מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה], in the first month [בָּרִאשׁוֹן], on the first of the month [בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ], the waters dried up [חָרְבוּ הַמַּיִם] from upon the earth [מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ]; and Noah removed [וַיָּסַר נֹחַ] the covering of the ark [אֶת-מִכְסֵה הַתֵּבָה], and he looked and saw [וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה] that the face of the ground was dry [חָרְבוּ פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה].

**8:14** And in the second month [וּבַחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי], on the twenty-seventh day of the month [בְּשִׁבְעָה וְעֶשְׂרִים יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ], the earth was dry [יָבְשָׁה הָאָרֶץ].

**8:15** And God spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר אֱלֹהִים] to Noah [אֶל-נֹחַ] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**8:16** Go out [צֵא] from the ark [מִן-הַתֵּבָה], you [אַתָּה] and your wife [וְאִשְׁתְּךָ] and your sons [וּבָנֶיךָ] and your sons' wives with you [וּנְשֵׁי-בָנֶיךָ אִתָּךְ].

**8:17** Every living thing [כָּל-הַחַיָּה] that is with you [אֲשֶׁר-אִתְּךָ] of all flesh [מִכָּל-בָּשָׂר] — of flying-things [בָּעוֹף] and of beasts [וּבַבְּהֵמָה] and of every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth [וּבְכָל-הָרֶמֶשׂ הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאָרֶץ] — bring out [הוֹצֵא] with you [אִתָּךְ]; and they shall swarm [וְשָׁרְצוּ] upon the earth [בָּאָרֶץ] and they shall be fruitful [וּפָרוּ] and multiply [וְרָבוּ] upon the earth [עַל-הָאָרֶץ].

**8:18** And Noah went out [וַיֵּצֵא נֹחַ], and his sons [וּבָנָיו] and his wife [וְאִשְׁתּוֹ] and his sons' wives with him [וּנְשֵׁי-בָנָיו אִתּוֹ].

**8:19** Every living thing [כָּל-הַחַיָּה], every creeping thing [כָּל-הָרֶמֶשׂ] and every flying-thing [וְכָל-הָעוֹף] — every moving thing upon the earth [כֹּל רוֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאָרֶץ] — went out [יָצְאוּ] from the ark [מִן-הַתֵּבָה] according to their families [לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתֵיהֶם].

**8:20** And Noah built [וַיִּבֶן נֹחַ] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] to YHWH [לַיהוָה], and he took [וַיִּקַּח] from every clean beast [מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהֹרָה] and from every clean flying-thing [וּמִכֹּל הָעוֹף הַטָּהוֹר], and he offered burnt offerings [וַיַּעַל עֹלֹת] upon the altar [עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ].

**8:21** And YHWH smelled [וַיָּרַח יְהוָה] the pleasing aroma [אֶת-רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ], and YHWH said in his heart [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-לִבּוֹ]: I will not again curse [לֹא-אֹסִף לְקַלֵּל] the ground [אֶת-הָאֲדָמָה] on account of the adam [בַּעֲבוּר הָאָדָם], for the inclination of the heart of the adam is evil [כִּי יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע] from his youth [מִנְּעֻרָיו]; and I will not again smite [וְלֹא-אֹסִף עוֹד לְהַכּוֹת] every living thing [אֶת-כָּל-חַי] as I have done [כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי].

**8:22** While all the days of the earth remain [עֹד כָּל-יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ] — seedtime and harvest [זֶרַע וְקָצִיר], and cold and heat [וְקֹר וָחֹם], and summer and winter [וְקַיִץ וָחֹרֶף], and day and night [וְיוֹם וָלָיְלָה] — shall not cease [לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 8:1 — וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ: "And God Remembered Noah"

This verse is the theological hinge of the entire flood narrative. Everything that follows — the subsiding of the waters, the exit from the ark, the covenant, the blessing — pivots on this single verb: זָכַר (zakar, "to remember").

**The verb זָכַר in biblical theology:** Divine remembering is not the recovery of forgotten information. YHWH does not forget and then recall. Rather, זָכַר in biblical usage is an act of engagement, of covenant-attention, of turning toward someone in loyal faithfulness. When God "remembers," he acts. The verb initiates narrative consequence:

- God remembered Rachel (Gen. 30:22) → she conceived
- God remembered his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Ex. 2:24) → the Exodus begins
- God remembered Hannah (1 Sam. 1:19) → she conceived Samuel
- Here: God remembered Noah → the wind comes, the waters subside

To say "God remembered Noah" is to say: God's covenant attention has turned toward Noah; the period of divine withdrawal (the letting-loose of the flood) is over; the initiative of re-creation begins. This is the turning point from de-creation to re-creation.

**The narrative structure:** The flood narrative is architecturally chiastic around this verse. Genesis 7:24 ("and the waters prevailed 150 days") is the nadir; 8:1 is the hinge; 8:3 ("and the waters decreased at the end of 150 days") is the rising mirror. The narrator frames the flood as a movement down to the maximum point of de-creation and then up through re-creation, with the divine memory as the center.

**"And all the living things and all the beasts with him in the ark":** The remembrance extends beyond Noah personally. The ark's complete cargo — the preserved remnant of the created order — is included in the divine remembering. God's covenant attention encompasses both the human remnant and the creature remnant. The ark is not merely Noah's lifeboat; it is the vessel of all creaturely life, and God's memory holds the whole.

**The ruach over the waters — echoing 1:2:** וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל-הָאָרֶץ — "And God caused a wind/spirit to pass over the earth." The word is רוּחַ (ruach), which in Genesis 1:2 is the "spirit of God" (רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים) that "hovered over the face of the waters" at the beginning of creation. The echo is unmistakable and deliberate:

| Gen 1:2 | Gen 8:1 |
|---|---|
| וְרוּחַ אֱלֹהִים מְרַחֶפֶת עַל-פְּנֵי הַמָּיִם | וַיַּעֲבֵר אֱלֹהִים רוּחַ עַל-הָאָרֶץ |
| "and the spirit/wind of God hovered over the face of the waters" | "and God caused a wind/spirit to pass over the earth" |

In Genesis 1, the ruach initiates the creation of ordered space; in Genesis 8, the ruach initiates the re-ordering of the flooded earth. The flood is reversed by the same creative breath that began the world. Re-creation and creation employ the same instrument.

**The verb שָׁכַךְ (shakak, "to subside/abate"):** וַיָּשֹׁכּוּ הַמַּיִם — "and the waters subsided." This verb is rare and specific: it means to subside from turbulence, to be stilled, to settle. It is the image of waters that had been violently agitated (the bursting of the deep, the opening of the windows) now becoming calm. The same root will describe the abating of anger (Esth. 2:1; 7:10) — passionate turbulence settling.

**LXX (Vaticanus) 8:1:** καὶ ἐμνήσθη ὁ θεὸς τοῦ Νωε καὶ πάντων τῶν θηρίων καὶ πάντων τῶν κτηνῶν καὶ πάντων τῶν πετεινῶν καὶ πάντων τῶν ἑρπετῶν — "And God remembered Noah and all the wild animals and all the cattle and all the birds and all the creeping things." The LXX expands the list of creature-categories to four, explicitly naming flying-things and creeping things that the MT subsumes under "all the living things." The core theological verb is faithfully rendered: ἐμνήσθη (emnēsthē, "he remembered") from μιμνῄσκομαι — the standard LXX translation of זָכַר.

---

### On 8:2 — The Sealing of the Cosmic Waters

וַיִּסָּכְרוּ מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמָיִם — "And the fountains of the deep were closed and the windows of the heavens."

This verse is the direct reversal of 7:11: נִבְקְעוּ כָּל-מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה וַאֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ — "All the fountains of the great deep burst open, and the windows of the heavens were opened."

| 7:11 (Opening) | 8:2 (Closing) |
|---|---|
| נִבְקְעוּ ("burst open") | וַיִּסָּכְרוּ ("were closed/sealed") |
| נִפְתָּחוּ ("were opened") | [implied closure] |

The verb סָכַר (sakar, "to shut, close, seal") is used. The fountains of the deep are sealed — the subterranean waters are reined in. The cosmological architecture is restored: the separation of waters above and below, the firmament as functional barrier, is reestablished. The rain (גֶּשֶׁם) is "restrained" (וַיִּכָּלֵא, from כָּלָא, "to hold back, restrain, withhold") — the word used for withholding rain in judgment contexts (1 Kgs. 8:35; Isa. 5:6).

The cosmic re-creation mirrors the original creation: just as at the beginning the waters were separated and held in their places by divine act, so now the released waters are recalled and re-contained.

---

### On 8:3-4 — The Flood Calendar: 150 Days and the Resting of the Ark

The calendar of the flood is one of the most precise chronological sequences in the primordial narrative. The 150 days that began in 7:24 (the waters prevailing) are now the 150 days of decrease (8:3). The symmetry is exact:

**The 150-day periods:**
- 7:11: 2nd month, 17th day → flood begins
- 7:24: waters prevail 150 days
- 8:3: waters decrease 150 days (the same period, now seen from the ark)
- 8:4: 7th month, 17th day → ark rests on Ararat

From the 17th day of the 2nd month to the 17th day of the 7th month = 5 months × 30 days = 150 days exactly. The calendar is a 30-day month system.

**הָרֵי אֲרָרָט — "the mountains of Ararat":** The Hebrew is unambiguously plural: הָרֵי (harey, construct plural of הַר, "mountain"). The ark rested upon the mountains of Ararat — a mountain range — not upon a single peak. "Mount Ararat" as a specific peak (the modern Ağrı Dağı in eastern Turkey, 5,137m) is a later identification tradition; the text itself says only that the ark came to rest somewhere in the Ararat mountain range (the ancient kingdom of Urartu, in modern Armenia/eastern Turkey/northwestern Iran). The LXX confirms this: ἐπὶ τὰ ὄρη τὰ Αραράτ — "upon the mountains of Ararat" (plural ὄρη).

**The verb נוּחַ (nuach, "to rest"):** וַתָּנַח הַתֵּבָה — "and the ark rested." This is the Noah-wordplay of the flood narrative carried to its culmination. Noah (נֹחַ) derives from the root נוּחַ ("rest, settle, be at rest"). The ark rests (תָּנַח) where Noah is. The name-bearer and the verb coincide: Noah is the man of rest, and the ark rests because Noah is in it. The wordplay first appeared in 5:29 (Noah will bring us "rest" from our labor) and reaches its completion here when the ark rests on the mountain.

---

### On 8:5 — Going and Decreasing: The Gerundive Construction

וְהַמַּיִם הָיוּ הָלוֹךְ וְחָסוֹר — "And the waters were going and decreasing."

The construction הָלוֹךְ וְחָסוֹר is an infinitive absolute pair expressing continuous, progressive action — "going and [continually] lessening." The same construction appeared in 8:3: הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב ("going and returning") describing the earlier phase of receding waters. These are the Bible's characteristic gerundive formations: infinitive absolute + finite verb (or parallel infinitives) expressing ongoing, intensifying process. Compare 1 Sam. 2:26: וְהַנַּעַר שְׁמוּאֵל הֹלֵךְ וְגָדֵל ("and the young man Samuel went on growing"). The waters did not drop in a sudden moment; they diminished progressively until the mountain-tops appeared.

**The appearance of the mountain-tops (8:5)** is the narrative's first sign of re-emerging dry land — the inverse of the creation moment when the dry land appeared (Gen. 1:9-10). The pattern of re-creation is now fully established: 8:1 (ruach over waters = 1:2), 8:2 (waters separated and contained = 1:6-9), 8:5 (dry land appearing = 1:9-10).

---

### On 8:6-12 — The Three Bird Missions: A Structural Reading

The raven and dove episodes (8:6-12) constitute a carefully structured sequence of three missions that together narrate the progressive return of habitable land.

**The structure:**

| Mission | Bird | Duration | Result | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | Raven | — | "going and returning" until waters dried | Land not yet dry |
| 2nd | Dove | — | Returns with no resting-place | Waters still on the earth |
| 3rd | Dove | +7 days | Returns with olive leaf | Waters lessening; vegetation alive |
| 4th (implicit) | Dove | +7 days | Does not return | Land accessible; new world habitable |

**The raven (עֹרֵב, orev):** A scavenger bird, the raven could sustain itself on floating carrion and debris. It "went out going and returning" (יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב) — the construction suggests it repeatedly left and returned without providing any diagnostic information about the state of the earth. The raven is ecologically indifferent to whether the land has dried; it can survive over water. It provides no intelligence.

**The dove (יוֹנָה, yonah):** A land bird, dependent on solid ground, seeds, and vegetation. The dove is the perfect instrument for detecting land conditions: it cannot rest on water, it requires vegetation, it returns to its keeper when it cannot find a resting-place. The dove's behavior is diagnostic of the earth's condition. Each return with/without a sign reads the state of the world.

**The escalating structure of the dove missions:** Three missions at seven-day intervals, each with a different outcome:
1. Returns with nothing — earth still covered
2. Returns with olive leaf — earth recovering, vegetation alive
3. Does not return — earth habitable, the dove has found a home

The narrative economy is perfect: three sendings, three outcomes, complete knowledge of the world's state achieved.

**Ancient Near Eastern parallels:** The Gilgamesh Epic (Tablet XI) also features a three-bird sequence (dove, swallow, raven) sent from the ship of Utnapishtim after the flood. The structural parallel is one of the strongest textual connections between the Hebrew flood narrative and the Mesopotamian tradition. Notably, the order of birds is different (Gilgamesh: dove, swallow, raven; Genesis: raven, then dove three times), and the significance differs: in Gilgamesh, the raven does not return because it found food (a positive sign); in Genesis, the raven's going and returning provides no information. The Hebrew narrative has restructured the borrowed motif with its own theological logic.

---

### On 8:7 — The Raven: יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב

וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב עַד-יְבֹשֶׁת הַמַּיִם מֵעַל הָאָרֶץ — "And it went out going and returning until the waters dried up from upon the earth."

The infinitive absolute construction יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב is the same gerundive pair used in 8:3 (הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב). Here it is applied to the raven's flight: a continuous back-and-forth until the conditions changed. The raven neither confirmed nor denied the state of the land — it simply kept flying between the ark and whatever landmasses or debris were available. Its flight is the image of the world in transition, not yet arrived anywhere.

---

### On 8:9 — The Dove's Return: The Hand Extended

וַיִּשְׁלַח יָדוֹ וַיִּקָּחֶהָ וַיָּבֵא אֹתָהּ אֵלָיו אֶל-הַתֵּבָה — "And he stretched out his hand and took her and brought her in to him into the ark."

The tenderness of this verse is striking. Noah reaches out his hand and takes the exhausted dove — מָנוֹחַ לְכַף-רַגְלָהּ ("no resting-place for the sole of her foot") — and brings her into the ark. The image of the hand extended to receive the bird that has found no rest is one of the most humanly intimate moments in the flood narrative. After chapters of cosmic catastrophe — the fountains of the deep, the waters prevailing, the total extinction of all land life — the narrative pauses on the gesture of a hand reaching out to receive a tired bird.

The verb שָׁלַח (shalach, "to stretch out, send, extend") is used here for Noah's hand (וַיִּשְׁלַח יָדוֹ) — the same root used for the sending of the bird itself (וַיְשַׁלַּח, vv. 7, 8, 10, 12). The hand that sends the bird is the hand that receives it.

**LXX 8:9:** καὶ ἐξέτεινεν τὴν χεῖρα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἔλαβεν αὐτήν καὶ εἰσήγαγεν αὐτὴν πρὸς ἑαυτὸν εἰς τὴν κιβωτόν — the LXX preserves this verse faithfully, including the three verbs: extended (ἐξέτεινεν), took (ἔλαβεν), brought in (εἰσήγαγεν).

---

### On 8:11 — The Olive Leaf: עֲלֵה-זַיִת טָרָף

וְהִנֵּה עֲלֵה-זַיִת טָרָף בְּפִיהָ — "And behold — a freshly plucked olive leaf in her mouth."

The word טָרָף (taraf) is critical and often mistranslated. It is an adjective or passive participle from the root טָרַף, which means "to tear, pluck, pull off." In its basic sense it describes something torn or freshly picked. Some translations render it "freshly plucked," others "a torn olive leaf." The force of the word is that the leaf is newly obtained — still fresh, still moist, still alive. This is not a dead leaf floating on receding waters; it is a leaf plucked from a living tree that the dove found while flying. The olive tree has survived; the earth is alive.

**The theological significance of the olive:** The olive is one of the great trees of the Land. Its appearance here — a living branch with a living leaf — signals not merely the recession of waters but the survival of vegetation, the renewal of the food chain, the possibility of human habitation. The olive requires years to grow and bear fruit; its survival through the flood implies the survival of deep-rooted vegetation. Life has returned below the surface.

**The olive and peace:** The olive branch became a universal symbol of peace and reconciliation, ultimately derived from this moment. The dove returning with the olive leaf becomes the iconic image of the flood's end — the end of divine judgment, the resumption of the created order, the beginning of the new world. The iconography is directly traceable to this verse.

**Wordplay — Noah/noach:** Like the name Noah (נֹחַ) and the ark's resting (נוּחַ, 8:4), the olive leaf carries the resonance of rest and the return of order. The dove finds no rest (מָנוֹחַ) in v. 9; the ark finds rest (וַתָּנַח) in v. 4; now the dove returns with a sign that the earth will once again offer rest to those upon it.

**LXX 8:11:** φύλλον ἐλαίας κάρφος — the LXX renders טָרָף as κάρφος ("a small piece, twig, splinter") rather than preserving the "freshly plucked" nuance. The LXX image is a twig of olive rather than a fresh-plucked leaf — a slight but notable shift in the concrete detail.

---

### On 8:13 — Noah Removes the Covering: וַיָּסַר נֹחַ אֶת-מִכְסֵה הַתֵּבָה

This is the first active human initiative in the re-creation sequence. Up to this point, Noah has sent birds; now he does something architecturally decisive: he removes the covering (מִכְסֵה, mikhseh) of the ark. The מִכְסֵה is the roof or covering that sealed the ark — the protective layer that kept the occupants in and the flood out. Removing it is the act of opening the sealed vessel to the new world.

**The date:** The first day of the first month of Noah's 601st year. This is the biblical New Year — the first of Nisan (or Aviv) — the month that will become the month of the Exodus (Ex. 12:2: "this month shall be for you the beginning of months, the first of the months of the year"). The uncovering of the ark on the first day of the new year carries the resonance of a new creation inaugurated at the turn of the year. The narrative is saturated with new-beginning symbolism.

**The two stages of drying:**
- 8:13: חָרְבוּ הַמַּיִם ("the waters dried up"), חָרְבוּ פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה ("the face of the ground was dry") — using חָרַב (harav, "to be dry, desolate, waste"), the word for a desolated or dried-up state
- 8:14: יָבְשָׁה הָאָרֶץ ("the earth was dry") — using יָבֵשׁ (yavesh, "to be dry"), the standard word for the dry land of creation (Gen. 1:9-10)

The two verbs represent two stages: first the water is gone (חָרַב) but the ground is not yet firm; then the earth is fully dry (יָבֵשׁ) and habitable. Noah waits for both before God commands the exit.

---

### On 8:15-17 — God's Exit Command: Re-Creation Language

The divine command to exit mirrors the divine command to enter (7:1: "Come, you and all your household, into the ark"). The symmetry is structural: the same household enters and exits; the same Elohim who commands here commands throughout the flood narrative's Priestly material.

**8:17 — "And they shall swarm / be fruitful / multiply":** וְשָׁרְצוּ בָּאָרֶץ וּפָרוּ וְרָבוּ עַל-הָאָרֶץ — these are the exact verbs of the creation blessing:

- שָׁרַץ (sharats, "to swarm, teem") — Gen. 1:20-21 (the creatures swarm in the waters)
- פָּרָה (parah, "to be fruitful") — Gen. 1:22, 1:28 (the blessing to creatures and to adam)
- רָבָה (ravah, "to multiply") — Gen. 1:22, 1:28 (the multiplication blessing)

The creation blessing is re-issued over the post-flood world. This is not merely a return to the pre-flood state; it is a re-creational moment, a second issuing of the first blessings. The same words that brought life into the world in Genesis 1 now re-commission the post-flood world.

**"According to their families" (לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתֵיהֶם, 8:19):** The exit is organized by family groupings. This is the first appearance of the word מִשְׁפָּחָה (mishpachah, "family, clan") in Genesis. The word will become central to the Table of Nations in Genesis 10 (the families of the earth spreading after the flood) and to the covenant with Abraham (18:18; 28:14: "all families of the earth will be blessed in you"). The post-flood re-peopling of the earth is structured by family — the social unit that carries life forward.

---

### On 8:20 — Noah's Altar: The First Altar in the Bible

וַיִּבֶן נֹחַ מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — "And Noah built an altar to YHWH."

This is the first mention of an altar (מִזְבֵּחַ, mizbeyach) in the Bible. The word derives from זָבַח (zavach, "to slaughter, sacrifice") — a place of slaughter-sacrifice. Noah's first act upon leaving the ark is not to survey the land, assess his situation, or build a shelter. It is to build an altar and offer sacrifice. The priority of worship over survival is the narrative's theological statement about the proper order of life in the new world.

**The burnt offering (עֹלָה, olah):** Noah offers עֹלֹת (burnt offerings) — the sacrifice in which the entire animal is consumed by fire, rising up (עָלָה, alah, "to go up") as smoke to God. Nothing is retained by the worshiper; the entire gift ascends. This will become the central sacrifice of the Levitical system (Lev. 1:1-17). Noah pre-figures the Sinaitic sacrificial order, as he pre-figured the clean/unclean distinction of the Torah (7:2).

**Clean animals and birds:** The surplus of clean animals (seven pairs taken in 7:2, versus two pairs of unclean) now reveals its purpose. The offering comes from the extra animals — the sacrificial surplus. The economy of the ark was planned for this moment. The seven pairs of clean animals were not merely for preservation of species; they were also for the first post-flood worship.

**The divine name YHWH:** The altar is built to יְהוָה (YHWH), not to אֱלֹהִים (Elohim). This is the relational name, the covenant name, the personal name. The sacrifice is offered to the God of personal relationship, not merely to the creator deity. The shift from the Priestly material of 8:15-19 (Elohim commands the exit) back to the YHWH-name for the sacrifice and divine response (8:20-22) is consistent with the YHWH-strand's characteristic focus on Noah's personal relationship with God.

**LXX 8:20:** καὶ ᾠκοδόμησεν Νωε θυσιαστήριον τῷ θεῷ — the LXX renders YHWH as τῷ θεῷ ("to God"), using the standard LXX practice of substituting θεός (God) or κύριος (Lord) for the Tetragrammaton. Here, unusually, the LXX uses θεός rather than κύριος, which would be the expected translation of YHWH. This may reflect a textual or theological leveling in the LXX tradition.

---

### On 8:21 — The Pleasing Aroma and the Reversal of Judgment

וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת-רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ — "And YHWH smelled the pleasing aroma."

**The Noah/noach wordplay reaches its climax here:** The name נֹחַ (Noah) echoes throughout the flood narrative via the root נוּחַ ("rest"):

- 5:29: Noah will bring us "rest" (נָחָם — relief/comfort, a near-pun on Noah's name)
- 8:4: וַתָּנַח הַתֵּבָה ("the ark rested") — נוּחַ
- 8:9: מָנוֹחַ ("a resting-place") — the dove finds none
- 8:21: רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ ("the pleasing aroma") — from נִיחוֹחַ, which is cognate to נוּחַ

The word נִיחֹחַ (nichoach) literally means "a soothing, a resting, a settling." The "pleasing aroma" is etymologically a "resting aroma" — the aroma that brings rest to God, that stills the divine agitation, that completes the settling. The entire Noah story is a story of rest: Noah as the man of rest, the ark resting on the mountains, the dove finding no rest but then the earth offering rest, and finally the sacrifice producing the resting aroma that soothes YHWH. The wordplay is architectural, spanning the entire narrative.

**"And YHWH said to his heart" (וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-לִבּוֹ):** This is divine interior monologue — YHWH speaking to himself, to his own heart. The phrase is rare and intimate. It is not proclamation or command but reflection. YHWH speaks within himself, formulating a resolution that will become the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9). The interiority of the divine speech marks this as a moment of genuine divine decision — not a decree imposed from outside but a commitment arising from the divine heart's own wrestling.

**"For the inclination of the heart of the adam is evil from his youth" — echo of 6:5 as the reason for mercy:**

The same diagnosis that provided the reason for the flood (6:5: וַיַּרְא יְהוָה כִּי רַבָּה רָעַת הָאָדָם — "And YHWH saw that the evil of the adam was great") now provides the reason for mercy. This is one of the most remarkable reversals in biblical theology:

| Gen 6:5 | Gen 8:21 |
|---|---|
| "every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day" | "for the inclination of the heart of the adam is evil from his youth" |
| → reason for JUDGMENT (the flood) | → reason for MERCY (never again) |

The same condition — the evil inclination of the human heart — that warranted total judgment in Genesis 6 now becomes the basis for unconditional mercy in Genesis 8. YHWH's logic does not conclude "therefore destroy again when humans sin again"; it concludes "therefore I will not destroy again, because humans will always be sinful, and destruction solves nothing." The flood was not a solution to human evil; it demonstrated the futility of using destruction as a corrective. YHWH chooses a different strategy: patient mercy, covenant commitment, forbearance in the face of persistent human failure.

This is the moral logic of grace: not that humanity deserved to be preserved (they did not — the evil inclination remains), but that YHWH chose to be committed to the world despite its persistent failure. The covenant that follows (Genesis 9) is unconditional precisely because the grounds for judgment are still present.

**יֵצֶר (yetzer, "inclination/formation"):** The word derives from יָצַר (yatsar, "to form, shape, fashion") — the same root used of YHWH forming (יִּיצֶר) the adam from the dust of the ground in Gen. 2:7. The יֵצֶר is the "formed tendency" of the human heart — the dispositional shape that the heart has been fashioned with. The evil inclination is, in some sense, part of the created structure of humanity. This becomes foundational to the later rabbinic concept of the יֵצֶר הָרָע (yetzer ha-ra, "the evil inclination") — the inbuilt human tendency toward self-serving that must be governed by Torah.

**DSS:** 1QS (Community Rule) from the Dead Sea Scrolls develops an extensive theology of the two יְצָרִים (inclinations) — the spirit of truth and the spirit of falsehood — rooted partly in this verse. The Qumran community's anthropology is deeply shaped by 8:21's acknowledgment of the persistent evil yetzer.

---

### On 8:22 — The Cosmic Covenant of Regularity

עֹד כָּל-יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ זֶרַע וְקָצִיר וְקֹר וָחֹם וְקַיִץ וָחֹרֶף וְיוֹם וָלָיְלָה לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ — "While all the days of the earth remain — seedtime and harvest, and cold and heat, and summer and winter, and day and night — shall not cease."

This verse is YHWH's inner vow translated into cosmic ordinance. The divine commitment of v. 21 is grounded in the natural order: the cycles of agricultural time, seasonal time, and daily time are the guarantees of the new order. YHWH's faithfulness is inscribed into the regularity of creation itself.

**The structure:** Three pairs of opposites:
1. **זֶרַע וְקָצִיר** (zera vekatsir) — seedtime and harvest: the agricultural cycle, the human food-production year
2. **קֹר וָחֹם** (qor vacham) — cold and heat: the temperature cycle, the physical climate
3. **קַיִץ וָחֹרֶף** (qayits vachoref) — summer and winter: the seasonal cycle
4. **יוֹם וָלָיְלָה** (yom valayla) — day and night: the daily cycle

The four pairs cover the full range of temporal regularity: daily, seasonal, agricultural, climatic. Together they constitute the promise that the world will remain ordered, predictable, hospitable — that the chaos of the flood (which disrupted all these cycles) will not return.

**לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ ("shall not cease"):** The verb שָׁבַת (shavat) is the root of שַׁבָּת (Shabbat). The cycles "shall not Sabbath" — shall not stop, rest, cease. There is a subtle irony in using the Sabbath-root for this vow: the Sabbath is the seventh-day rest built into creation (Gen. 2:1-3); here YHWH promises that the non-Sabbath cycles — the working cycles of day and night, season and harvest — will never themselves take a Sabbath, never permanently rest. Creation's restlessness (day following night, harvest following seedtime) is the guarantee of the new order.

**The Noahic covenant as creation covenant:** This verse and the Noahic covenant of chapter 9 together constitute what theologians call the "covenant of creation" or the "Noahic covenant" — a covenant with all of humanity and all creation (not just with Israel), unconditional in character, with the rainbow as its sign (9:12-17). The covenant is universal, extending beyond Israel to every nation and every creature. It is the foundational covenant upon which all other covenants rest.

**LXX 8:22:** In the LXX Vaticanus, this verse is rendered as a straightforward conditional: πάσας τὰς ἡμέρας τῆς γῆς σπόρος καὶ θερισμός, ψῦχος καὶ καῦμα, θέρος καὶ ἔαρ, ἡμέραν καὶ νύκτα οὐ καταπαύσουσιν — "all the days of the earth: sowing and reaping, cold and heat, summer and spring, day and night, shall not cease." The LXX substitutes ἔαρ ("spring") for the MT's חֹרֶף ("winter") — using the spring/summer pair rather than the winter/summer pair. This is either a different reading of the Hebrew or a deliberate softening: the LXX prefers the spring-summer pair to the winter-summer pair, perhaps reflecting a different agricultural or seasonal frame of reference.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 8

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8:1 | וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ ("And God remembered Noah") | καὶ ἐμνήσθη ὁ θεὸς τοῦ Νωε ("And God remembered Noah") | LXX faithfully renders the pivotal verb; expands animal categories |
| 8:1 | רוּחַ ("wind/spirit") | πνεῦμα ("wind/spirit") | LXX preserves the ruach/pneuma resonance with 1:2 |
| 8:4 | עַל הָרֵי אֲרָרָט ("upon the mountains of Ararat") | ἐπὶ τὰ ὄρη τὰ Αραράτ ("upon the mountains of Ararat") | Both MT and LXX correctly use plural "mountains" |
| 8:11 | עֲלֵה-זַיִת טָרָף ("freshly plucked olive leaf") | φύλλον ἐλαίας κάρφος ("a twig of olive") | LXX loses the "freshly plucked" nuance; renders as a twig |
| 8:20 | מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה ("altar to YHWH") | θυσιαστήριον τῷ θεῷ ("altar to God") | LXX uses θεός rather than κύριος — unusual substitution for YHWH |
| 8:21 | רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ ("the pleasing/resting aroma") | ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας ("smell of a sweet odor") | LXX loses the niach/Noah wordplay entirely |
| 8:21 | וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-לִבּוֹ ("YHWH said to his heart") | καὶ εἶπεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς διανοηθείς ("and the Lord God thought/reflected") | LXX adds "God" alongside "Lord," uses a cognitive verb rather than the intimate "said to his heart" |
| 8:21 | יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו ("the inclination of the heart of the adam is evil from his youth") | ἡ διάνοια τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐπιμελῶς ἐπὶ τὰ πονηρὰ ἐκ νεότητος ("the thought of man is diligently upon evil things from youth") | LXX renders יֵצֶר as διάνοια ("thought/mind") — loses the "yetzer" formation-concept crucial to later Jewish theology |
| 8:22 | קַיִץ וָחֹרֶף ("summer and winter") | θέρος καὶ ἔαρ ("summer and spring") | LXX substitutes "spring" for "winter" — different seasonal pairing |
